title: SEGMENT 3 — The early hits: insulin, growth hormone, and the founding of biotech

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The early hits: insulin, growth hormone, and the founding of biotech
I want to take you back to 1976, because the company that started all of this was founded in 1976, and the story is worth telling in some detail.

The company is Genentech, founded by Bob Swanson, a venture capitalist, and Herb Boyer, a UCSF biochemist who, with Stanley Cohen at Stanford, had co-invented recombinant DNA technology a few years earlier.
Swanson cold-called Boyer, the story goes, and convinced him to meet for a beer.
The conversation, by all accounts, lasted hours.
Swanson asked: can we commercialize this technology?
Boyer said: yes.

They went and founded the company on what was, at the time, a wildly speculative bet — that you could take a human gene, put it onto a bacterial plasmid, transform E. coli, and have the bacteria produce a human protein drug in commercial quantities.
Nobody had done that.
Nobody knew if it was possible.
The whole project was an extended bet that the plasmid and recombinant DNA tools we covered in the plasmids lecture would scale into a manufacturing process.
The first product they picked was a small, well-characterized hormone, partly because they had to pick something the science could plausibly handle.
They picked somatostatin — a 14-amino-acid peptide — as a proof of concept, and in 1977 they showed they could make it in E. coli.
That was the demonstration.
Then they went after the real prize.
Insulin.
Insulin, at the time, was extracted from the pancreases of slaughtered pigs and cows.
The supply was tied to the meat industry.
The product was animal insulin, slightly different from human insulin in amino acid sequence, which caused immune reactions in some patients.
There were real, looming concerns about supply — diabetic populations were growing, and the meat industry could not necessarily keep pace.
A recombinant source of pure human insulin, made by bacteria, would be a transformative product.
So Genentech set out to make it.

The team — including a young David Goeddel — chemically synthesized the gene for human insulin, since the natural gene was a nightmare of cloning at the time, broke the protein into its two chains, expressed each chain separately in E. coli, harvested them, and joined them chemically.
In 1978, they announced that they had produced human insulin in E. coli.
The first recombinant protein drug had been made.
The commercial partner was Eli Lilly, who had decades of expertise in insulin manufacturing.
Lilly took the Genentech process, scaled it, and in 1982 the product — Humulin — received FDA approval.
Recombinant human insulin became the first biotech drug ever sold.
Type 1 diabetic patients started transitioning off pig insulin and onto Humulin.
The bottleneck of the meat-supply chain was gone.
The price came down.
The supply scaled.

As of today, virtually all insulin used in the world is recombinant, made in either E. coli or yeast, with manufacturing capacity that vastly exceeds what could ever have been extracted from animal pancreases.
I want you to sit with what that means.
Recombinant insulin was the first proof that you could replace an entire extractive industry with engineered microbes.
The pig-pancreas industry, with all its slaughterhouses and abattoirs and seasonal supply variations, was replaced by a fermenter.
The fermenter produced a purer product.
It produced more of it.
It produced it on demand.
It produced human insulin, not pig insulin, so the immune reactions went away.
This was not just a new drug.
It was a new paradigm.
And the paradigm was: take any protein you want, find the gene, put it on a plasmid, put it into a microbe, scale up.
That paradigm is the foundation on which the entire biotech industry was built.
The next big product was human growth hormone.

Before recombinant DNA, human growth hormone was extracted from the pituitary glands of human cadavers — a horrifying logistical process, and one that was eventually shut down in 1985 when several patients developed Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, the human version of mad cow, traced to prion contamination in the cadaver-derived hormone.
The need for a recombinant alternative was, at that point, urgent.
Genentech had been working on it in parallel and was ready.
In 1985, recombinant human growth hormone, marketed as Protropin, received FDA approval.
Kids with pituitary dwarfism could now be treated with a safe, abundant supply.

Then came a wave of products — tissue plasminogen activator for stroke, erythropoietin for anemia, interferon alpha for hepatitis and certain cancers, factor VIII for hemophilia, dozens of other proteins.
Each one was a gene, put on a plasmid or integrated into a chromosome, expressed in either E. coli or yeast or, for the more complex glycosylated proteins, in mammalian CHO cells.
By the early 1990s, the recombinant protein industry was a multi-billion-dollar business, and it had created the template for what metabolic engineering would do next.
The thing I want you to notice about all of these early hits is that they were proteins.
You picked a gene, you expressed the gene, you harvested the protein.
There was, in some sense, only one enzyme that mattered — the one encoded by your gene of interest.
The cell's own metabolism was, for the most part, irrelevant.
You were using the cell as a translation machine for one specific gene.
That is not really metabolic engineering, in the deeper sense.
That is heterologous protein production.
And the language reflects that — the field at the time was often called "recombinant DNA technology" or "biopharmaceutical manufacturing," not "metabolic engineering.
The next step — the step that turned this into metabolic engineering proper — was to ask: what if the product is not a protein?

What if the product is a small molecule, like a drug or a chemical, and to make it you have to install not one enzyme but a whole pathway of enzymes, and you have to redirect the cell's metabolic flux through that pathway?
That is a fundamentally different problem.
That is metabolic engineering in the strict sense.
And the project that, more than any other, established what that looked like was Jay Keasling's artemisinin yeast.
We turn to that next.
